preamble: The transformation from a voiceless aspirated alveolar plosive /tʰ/ to a voiceless retroflex affricate /ʈ͡ʂ/ is a common phonetic process observed in various dialects of English, particularly in North American English. This shift, often referred to as affrication and retroflexion, occurs when the alveolar plosive /t/ (typically aspirated as [tʰ] in stressed initial syllables) precedes a retroflex approximant /r/. Coarticulation causes the tongue to anticipate the retroflex position of the /r/, leading to the production of a retroflex affricate.

examples.note: The aspirated alveolar plosive /tʰ/ undergoes affrication and retroflexion when preceding a retroflex approximant /r/ (often realized as [ɻʷ]), resulting in a voiceless retroflex affricate [ʈ͡ʂ].

sources: Baker, A., & Mielke, J. (2022). Affrication as the cause of /s/-retraction: Evidence from Manchester English. Glossa: A Journal of General Linguistics, 7(1).
